questions.question: Which approach is most efficient for expensive-to-train models?
questions.explanation: Bayesian optimization builds a probabilistic model of the objective, focusing evaluations on promising regions.

questions.question: Cross-validation during hyperparameter search helps:
options: Avoid overfitting to a single validation split
questions.explanation: Each configuration is evaluated on multiple folds, giving a more reliable estimate of generalisation performance.

questions.question: In k-fold cross-validation:
options: Each fold serves as test set exactly once while others train
questions.explanation: K-fold creates k equal-sized folds. Each fold is the test set once; the model is trained on the remaining k-1 folds.

questions.question: Using the same test set repeatedly during hyperparameter search causes:
options: Data leakage into the tuning process
questions.explanation: The search algorithm can overfit to the test set, selecting hyperparameters that happen to work well on that specific set but not in general.

questions.question: sklearn Pipeline ensures:
options: Scaler/preprocessor fits only on training folds — preventing leakage
questions.explanation: Without Pipeline, scaling on all data before CV leaks test statistics into training. Pipeline fixes this automatically.

questions.question: Nested cross-validation is used to:
options: Get an unbiased estimate of the model's generalisation performance after tuning
questions.explanation: Nested CV has an outer loop for evaluation and inner loop for tuning — keeping the outer test set truly unseen.

questions.question: Optuna's suggest_float('lr', 1e-4, 1e-1, log=True) samples from:
options: A log-uniform distribution — concentrating samples at smaller values
questions.explanation: log=True samples multiplicatively (like 1e-4, 3e-4, 1e-3 ...) rather than linearly, appropriate for learning rates spanning decades.

questions.question: Random Search outperforms Grid Search when:
options: Only a few hyperparameters matter — random search focuses budget on those
questions.explanation: If only 2 of 10 hyperparameters matter, grid search wastes evaluations on irrelevant combinations. Random search explores the important dimensions more effectively.

questions.question: Halving strategies (Successive Halving) work by:
options: Eliminating the worst-performing configurations each round, allocating more budget to survivors
questions.explanation: Successive Halving quickly discards poor configurations, spending compute only on promising ones.

questions.question: The 'warm start' parameter in sklearn estimators allows:
options: Using previous training for initialization, speeding up retraining
questions.explanation: warm_start=True reuses the fitted model from the previous call as initialization, useful when incrementally adding estimators.
